Hey, what's going on guys? I just wanted to give you a quick kind of overview of this pitch. So some of you guys already saw it because you saw it live if you're new to Jim Moritz welcome. But the actual pitch itself follows a really, really simple framework. And the way that breaking beliefs and selling really works is there's basically four main things that have to happen. So first is you're going to state a belief that is incorrect, right? Then you're going to break that belief by saying why it is wrong. Then you're going to give them a new belief to hold on to explain why it is right and then provide proof. Those five steps say what there's was why it's wrong, say what's right, why it's right, proof. That's the formula. That's how you break beliefs. And then within the context of a group pitch, you want to have one main thing that we're convincing of and then usually three supporting arguments that tend to be internal, external, and then kind of a reinforcement of the vehicle itself. In our instance, it's going to be fitness, nutrition and accountability. That's kind of how we tie this together. The main thing is that you need all of them together. That's our one main thing we're convincing of is that they've had things in the past. They had one or two or half of one, but they never had all three together. That's the one main thing we're trying to make them believe. And because we have all three, they will buy. And then with each of these three, this is the cardio, sorry, this is the fitness belief breaking. So the false belief that they're coming in with is that they think that cardio or diolone will help them with permanent weight loss. And what we're breaking there is that you need to have resistance training. You need to be training your muscles while you're losing weight so that you can maintain as much muscle mass as possible so that you don't decrease your metabolism. That's the idea. That's the false beliefs that we're breaking. We replace that belief by explaining to them that that's what happens when they lose weight versus losing fat. And that's why they continue to rebound. And then it's a boom belief is broken. And then the proof there is their experience. And then before and after is intestinal loss. That's false belief. I just want like the pitch is much longer than this obviously. But just to understand the general framework of what it is, it's like, here's the main thing, they need all three together. Boom, that's the big point. False belief number one, your exercise can't just be cardio. It has to have resistance training. That's why I've been failing in the past. But false people to is around nutrition. They think they need a diet or just to cut out a certain food group. When in fact, it has to be a long term solution has to include all the groups and it has to be fun to stick with. That's what we're breaking from a belief standpoint that is that diets don't work. It has to be a long term solution. And they can just cut out food groups, right? And intrinsically, a lot of people will admit they kind of know this, but they didn't really follow it. So it's actually very easy to lean on them for that because it makes sense. So the proof there is their experience again, that's why they have an integral stick with it. And then the results, right? More testimonials. False belief number three is that they can do it on their own. They're like, well, you know, if I have the right if I have a workout plan and I have a diet, I can do this on my own. And you need to replace that belief that they need outside help to give them objective, objective feedback, make tweaks and make them actually stick with it. And I like showing the Olympic athlete as an example, because it just makes sense. People out of time have their egos like, I'm going to coach the cycle, the best in the world of coaches, why would you not have a coach? And then, you know, like, they also think maybe I could just do with a friend and we break that belief as well. So we're really trying to break off any of their thoughts like, well, I could maybe do it on my own, maybe I could get my friend to do it. No, neither of those will work. You need an outside coach for a variety of reasons, and you need some social support. So we break all three of those. And then it's like, okay, you need fitness, nutrition, accountability, the fitness you were doing was wrong, the nutrition you were doing was wrong, the accountability you're doing was wrong. Here's why you should sign up. And then we give them a great offer, urgency, scarcity, and close it up. Hopefully that makes sense. I just like to kind of explain the underworking is kind of like the skeleton behind this whole pitch. So that it makes sense. So that in the back of your mind, you're like, did I really do a good job of explaining belief number one, belief number two, belief number three. And whenever we do these, we try and give stories or sorts of the day, analogies, parables, whatever you want to say to illustrate those things, because people remember them and they work a lot better. So if you can't leave those in personal, anecdotal, whatever, all those things work and will enhance the power of the pitch.
